title: Testimony: Are Digital Services Taxes a Viable Solution for the EU Budget?
summary: Digital services taxes address a real concern—the need to adapt taxation to the digital economy—but they are not the right solution. They raise limited revenue, are often passed on to consumers rather than large digital firms, create economic distortions, increase complexity and compliance costs, negatively impact innovation and competitiveness, and risk international retaliation.
ai_summary: This testimony critically assesses the viability of Digital Services Taxes (DSTs) as a funding mechanism for the EU budget, arguing against their effectiveness. The analysis highlights that DSTs yield limited revenue, impose high compliance costs, and often create economic distortions without effectively targeting large digital firms, risking international trade disputes.

title: The European Court of Human Rights has upheld the weaponisation of privacy to restrict tax authorities’ access to banking data

ai_summary: A study from the Fraser Institute concludes that the United States tax code is significantly progressive when compared to other OECD countries. This indicates that higher-income individuals in the US contribute a larger proportion of their income in taxes relative to their counterparts in other developed nations.

title: HMRC’s GfC13: why governance is paramount for taxpayers and advisers - International Tax Review
summary: HMRC’s GfC13: why governance is paramount for taxpayers and advisers International Tax Review
ai_summary: HMRC's GfC13 guidance emphasizes the critical importance of robust governance frameworks for both taxpayers and their advisers in the UK. This initiative aims to enhance tax compliance and mitigate risks through improved internal controls and transparency in tax matters.
